figures.caption: Figure 3. (A-C) MA plots looking at allele-biased expression between the X and X’ allele within gynogenic 219 females, in germline, somatic reproductive tissue, and somatic non-reproductive tissue, respectively. The x-axes 220 shows average expression of the gene in three replicates while the y-axes shows the log2 of the difference in 221 expression between the two alleles. Values on the y-axes >0 shows X’-biased expression, while <0 shows X-biased 222 expression. Genes with significant allele-biased expression are coloured in according to the direction of bias 223 (maroon for X’, light pink for X). While most X-linked genes in gynogenic females have X-biased expression, there 224

Table 2. Summary table of the number of X-linked genes with allele-biased expression in gynogenic females, in 205 germline, somatic reproductive, and somatic non-reproductive tissue. Unexpressed genes and genes whose models 206 failed to converge are not included. 207

Figure 4. (A-C) Principal component analysis of autosomal gene expression in germline, somatic reproductive 268 tissue, and somatic non-reproductive tissue, respectively. Samples show clustering first by male (blue) vs female, 269 with subsequent clustering by female reproductive morph (purple for gynogenic, green for androgenic). (D-F) 270 Volcano plots demonstrating differential gene expression in germline, reproductive tissue, and somatic non-271 reproductive tissue, respectively. The x-axis is the log fold-change of gene expression between gynogenic and 272 androgenic females, using androgenic females as a baseline. Values >0 indicate gynogenic-biased expression, while 273 values <0 indicate androgenic-biased expression. The y-axis shows the -log10 of the false discovery rate (FDR), a p-274 value adjusted for multiple testing. The grey horizontal line is drawn at -log10(0.05), and genes above that line are 275 coloured (purple for gynogenic and green for androgenic) to signify significance. 276

Figure 6. (A, E) Principal component analysis of maternal RNA deposited into eggs, for X-linked (X and X’) genes 325 (A) and autosomal genes (E). In both cases, samples show clustering by female reproductive morph. (B, F) Volcano 326 plot of maternal RNA deposits, for X-linked genes (B) and autosomal genes (F). The x-axis is the log fold-change of 327 gene expression between gynogenic and androgenic females, using androgenic females as a baseline. Values >0 328 indicate gynogenic-biased expression, while values <0 indicate androgenic-biased expression. The y-axis shows the 329 -log10 of the false discovery rate (FDR), a p-value adjusted for multiple testing. The grey horizontal line is drawn at -330 log10(0.05), and genes above that line are coloured (purple for gynogenic and green for androgenic) to signify 331 significance. (C) A MA plot showing allele-biased expression between the X and X’ allele in gynogenic female 332 maternal deposits, using data from eggs pre-determined to develop as females. The x-axes shows average expression 333 of the gene in three replicates while the y-axes shows the log2 of the difference in expression between the two 334 alleles. Values on the y-axes >0 shows X’-biased expression, while <0 shows X-biased expression. Genes with 335 significant allele-biased expression are coloured in according to the direction of bias (purple for X’, green for X). 336 (D) Scatterplot showing the correlation relationship between differential expression in genes between female 337 reproductive morphs, and allele-biased expression between X and X’ gametologs in gynogenic females, in maternal 338 RNA deposit. 339 340

Table 3. Summary table of the number of autosomal genes with significant differential expression between 258 androgenic and gynogenic females. This is shown separately for germline, somatic reproductive, and somatic non-259 reproductive tissue. 260

Table 1. Summary table of the number of X-linked (X or X’) genes with significant differential expression between 170 androgenic and gynogenic females. This is shown separately for germline, somatic reproductive, and somatic non-171 reproductive tissue. 172

Table 4. Summary table of differential gene expression in maternal RNA deposits into eggs, for X-linked and 320 autosomal genes. Also allele-biased expression for X and X’ gametologs within the maternal deposits of gynogenic 321 females. 322

Figure 7. Methods to differentiate between female reproductive morph in the lab and wild line. A.) Holo2 wing 485 markers in a lab stock of B. coprophila. Gynogenic (X’X) females carry a dominant wavy marker (W) on the X’ 486 chromosome, which is not present in androgenic (XX) females. Males are phenotypically different from females, 487 most prominently by the presence of a pair of posterior claspers (not pictured). B.) Molecular assay using primer 488 DEL00003018 to differentiate between gynogenic and androgenic females in a wild line with no wing markers. The 489 primer selectively amplifies a region of the X chromosome where the homologous region on the X’ houses a ~500bp 490 deletion. Therefore, one long fragment is amplified from XX females, while two DNA fragments of different sizes 491 are amplified from X’X females. 492 493

Figure 2. (A-C) Principal component analysis of X-linked gene expression in germline, somatic reproductive tissue, 175 and somatic non-reproductive tissue, respectively. Samples cluster first by male (blue) vs female, with subsequent 176 clustering by female reproductive morph (purple for gynogenic, green for androgenic). (D-F) Volcano plots 177 demonstrating differential gene expression in germline, reproductive tissue, and somatic non-reproductive tissue, 178 respectively. The x-axis is the log fold-change of gene expression between gynogenic and androgenic females, using 179 androgenic females as a baseline. Values >0 indicate gynogenic-biased expression, while values <0 indicate 180 androgenic-biased expression. The y-axis shows the -log10 of the false discovery rate (FDR), a p-value adjusted for 181 multiple testing. The grey horizontal line is drawn at -log10(0.05), and genes above that line are coloured (purple for 182 gynogenic and green for androgenic) to signify significance. 183 184

Figure 5. Plots for average male expression against average female expression for all genes (A, D, G), autosomal 295 genes only (B, E, H), and X/X’ genes only (C, F, I); for germline (A-C), somatic reproductive tissue (D-F), and 296 somatic non-reproductive tissue (G-I). Female expression is further divided into androgenic (green) and gynogenic 297 (purple) expression. Regression lines are drawn between male and female expressions separately for the two female 298 morphs. There is no significant difference in the regression lines of the two morphs in any group of genes in any 299 tissue, suggesting that there is no difference in relationship between how expression in the two reproductive morphs 300 compare to male expression. 301 302
